author: Dr. Sandra Kim, MD
content:
URGENT CARE VISIT — 9/30/2025, 4:20 PM
Patient: Nguyen, Lan Thi (Age 71)
Referring source: Patient/family walk-in via Hopkins Bayview Urgent Care
Interpreter: Hopkins Vietnamese phone interpreter throughout.

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
RUQ abdominal pain after dinner last night and again this afternoon.

HPI:
71-year-old Vietnamese-speaking woman, retired textile factory manager, brought to urgent care by son Quang. She describes intermittent severe RUQ abdominal pain that started ~24 hours ago after a heavier-than-usual dinner (her daughter-in-law's birthday dinner with rich foods including grilled pork belly and coconut-milk-based dishes). The pain came on ~1 hour after eating, severe and crampy in nature, radiated to her back, lasted about 2 hours, then resolved spontaneously. She slept poorly. Today around lunchtime she had another episode after a fattier-than-typical lunch (leftovers); same character, ~90 minutes, again resolved. She is currently pain-free in the visit but anxious about further episodes. Reports mild persistent nausea between attacks. No vomiting. No fever. No diarrhea. No jaundice noted by family.

Reports she has actually had 2 or 3 milder, briefer episodes of similar pain over the past year that she did not seek care for — she had assumed they were related to overeating.

Denies: hematemesis, melena, BRBPR, hematuria, chest pain, dyspnea, fever, chills, recent weight loss.

PMH:
- HTN (lisinopril, well-controlled)
- HLD (atorvastatin, at goal)
- CKD stage 2/3a (Cr ~1.3 most recently; up from 1.2 in 2022; on close PCP follow-up)
- s/p Right TKA 2018
- Latent TB infection (untreated, asymptomatic)
- Allergies: PCN (childhood rash); iodinated contrast (urticarial reaction 2019 during CT for knee evaluation — has been pre-medicated since)
- Mild osteopenia

MEDS:
- Lisinopril 10 mg PO daily
- Atorvastatin 40 mg PO daily
- Calcium + vit D supplement
- Acetaminophen PRN
- Ibuprofen 200–400 mg PRN (markedly reduced since 2022 CKD discussion)

VITALS: BP 148/86, HR 96, RR 16, T 98.2, SpO2 98% RA. Weight 63.2 kg.

EXAM:
General: Mildly anxious, in NAD currently. Pain-free now.
HEENT: Sclera anicteric. Conjunctiva pink.
CV: RRR, no murmur.
Lungs: CTAB.
Abdomen: Soft, NT to palpation in all four quadrants currently. No rebound, no guarding. Murphy sign cannot be elicited as patient pain-free. Mildly TTP at RUQ (subjectively voluntary guarding). No palpable mass. No organomegaly. Bowel sounds normal.
Skin: No jaundice. No spider angiomata, no caput.
MSK: Right knee well-healed, no issues.

LABS (drawn in urgent care):
- CBC: WBC 6.8, Hgb 12.1, Plt 256 — normal
- BMP: Na 139, K 4.0, BUN 19, Cr 1.3 (eGFR 41, CKD-EPI), Glu 88 — Cr stable, eGFR borderline 3a
- LFTs: AST 28, ALT 31, ALP 78, T-bili 0.9, Albumin 4.1 — all normal
- Lipase 38 — normal
- Urine HCG: N/A (post-menopausal)

IMAGING:
- ABDOMINAL ULTRASOUND (urgent care US tech available; pre-medication for contrast not needed for US):
  • Liver: normal size and echotexture. No focal lesions.
  • GALLBLADDER: distended; CONTAINS MULTIPLE STONES (gallstones, with associated shadowing); wall not thickened (2 mm); no pericholecystic fluid; no sonographic Murphy sign currently.
  • Common bile duct: 5 mm (within normal limits for her age).
  • Pancreas: incompletely visualized due to bowel gas (limited).
  • Right kidney: normal size and cortex; no hydronephrosis.
  • Aorta: normal caliber.
  IMPRESSION: Cholelithiasis without acute cholecystitis. No biliary obstruction or pancreatitis on this study.

IMPRESSION / PLAN:
1. SYMPTOMATIC CHOLELITHIASIS — biliary colic pattern. Currently pain-free. No findings of acute cholecystitis (no fever, no leukocytosis, normal LFTs, no GB wall thickening on US).
2. Counseled patient and son extensively (through interpreter):
   - Strong recommendation for elective cholecystectomy referral. Symptomatic gallstones recur with increasing frequency and risk progressing to cholecystitis, choledocholithiasis, gallstone pancreatitis, or cholangitis. Discussed risks (cost, surgical recovery in a 71-year-old, anesthesia) vs benefits (definitive cure).
   - Patient is somewhat hesitant given her positive experience with the TKA in 2018 but concern about another surgery and longer downtime. She will discuss with son.
   - Provided referral to General Surgery at Hopkins Bayview (Dr. Markovich) for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy consideration.
   - DIETARY: low-fat diet starting today; avoid fried/greasy foods and large meals.
3. Return precautions (Vietnamese written): fever, chills, jaundice (yellowing of eyes or skin), persistent severe pain >6 hours, vomiting, dark urine, clay-colored stools.
4. PCP follow-up with Dr. Le in 2 weeks to coordinate surgery referral decision.

Visit time: 95 minutes (including imaging) — 35 min interpreter time.
attestation:
Electronically signed by: Dr. Sandra Kim, MD
Hopkins Bayview Urgent Care
Date: 9/30/2025
